content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 10, 1990


Honorable Gary Lemke
Supervisor, Lassen County
County Courthouse
Susanville, CA 96130

Re: Your Request for Informal Advice
Our File No. I-90-226

Dear Mr. Lemke:

You are seeking advice regarding your duties and responsibil-
ities as an elected member of the Lassen County Board of Supervi-
sors regarding the permissible use of campaign funds provisions of
the Political Reform Act ("the Act").¹ Because your request for
advice does not refer to a specific governmental decision, we are
treating your question as a request for informational assistance only,
pursuant to Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed).²

The following advice is based upon the facts provided in your
letter of February 5, 1990, which was only recently received by
this office.

QUESTION

Can you use existing campaign funds to purchase office equipment,
such as a computer, for use in your duties as county supervisor?
Can such funds also be used to send letters to your district's constituents?

CONCLUSION

Subject to certain conditions and restrictions, existing campaign funds can be used for the purchase of a computer or other


¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. I-90-226
Page 2

office equipment for use in your duties as county supervisor.
Campaign funds may be used to send letters to your constituents.

FACTS

You are an elected member of the Lassen County Board of
Supervisors, elected to your current term in June, 1988. You have
less than $2,000 left in your campaign account and have neither
received nor spent any funds since 1988.

ANALYSIS

Commencing with Section 85800, the Act specifies various lim-
itations on permitted uses of campaign funds. We respond to your
questions concerning specific uses of campaign funds in the fol-
lowing discussion, and refer you to Sections 85800 - 85807 for
other questions you may have with regard to permitted uses of cam-
paign funds in general.

As a general rule, an expenditure of campaign funds is per-
mitted when reasonably related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. If the expenditure confers a "substantial
personal benefit," it is permitted only when directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 85801.)

The Act permits the use of campaign funds to purchase "any
appliance or equipment" only where the legal title resides in the
campaign committee. (Section 85805(a); see also Viramonte Advice
Letter, No. A-90-051, copy enclosed.) When title resides in the
campaign committee, the purchase is considered to be directly re-
lated to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose provid-
ed the use of the appliance or equipment for "other purposes" is
"only incidental." (Section 85805(c).)

Under this section, therefore, two requirements must be sat-
isfied in order for the office equipment to be purchased with your
campaign funds. First, title to the purchased office equipment
must be in the campaign committee's name and cannot be in your
name, or the name of a member of your immediate family, or in the
name of the committee treasurer. Second, any use of the office
equipment other than for a permitted political, legislative, or
governmental purpose can only be incidental to such permitted
uses.

The Act permits the use of campaign funds for mailings to
constituents. Expenditures to seek office that are reasonably
related to a political purpose, and expenditures associated with
holding office that are reasonably related to a legislative or
governmental purpose, are in compliance with the "public trust"
provisions of Section 85202.3 (Section 85801.)

_________________________
3 "All contributions ... shall be deemed to be held in trust for
expenses associated with the election of the candidate to the
specific office ...." (Section 85202(b).)

File No. I-90-226
Page 3


     Depending on the circumstances, sending "information letters"
to constituents in your supervisorial district would appear to
constitute activity that was either related to seeking (reelection
to) office or otherwise associated with holding office.
     While the Act prohibits the use of public funds to send many of
these types of mailings (Section 89001; Regulation 18901 (copy
enclosed)), it contemplates the use of campaign funds for such
purposes.

     I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this mat-
ter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Kathryn E. Donovan
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Jonathan S. Rothman
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

KED:JSR:plh

Enclosures

Subject to certain conditions and restrictions, existing campaign funds can be used for the purchase of a computer or other

sections.facts: You are an elected member of the Lassen County Board of Supervisors, elected to your current term in June, 1988. You have less than $2,000 left in your campaign account and have neither received nor spent any funds since 1988.
sections.analysis:
Commencing with Section 85800, the Act specifies various limitations on permitted uses of campaign funds. We respond to your questions concerning specific uses of campaign funds in the following discussion, and refer you to Sections 85800 - 85807 for other questions you may have with regard to permitted uses of campaign funds in general.

As a general rule, an expenditure of campaign funds is permitted when reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. If the expenditure confers a "substantial personal benefit," it is permitted only when directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 85801.)

The Act permits the use of campaign funds to purchase "any appliance or equipment" only where the legal title resides in the campaign committee. (Section 85805(a); see also Viramontes Advice Letter, No. A-90-051, copy enclosed.) When title resides in the campaign committee, the purchase is considered to be directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose provided the use of the appliance or equipment for "other purposes" is "only incidental." (Section 85805(c).)

Under this section, therefore, two requirements must be satisfied in order for the office equipment to be purchased with your campaign funds. First, title to the purchased office equipment must be in the campaign committee's name and cannot be in your name, or the name of a member of your immediate family, or in the name of the committee treasurer. Second, any use of the office equipment other than for a permitted political, legislative, or governmental purpose can only be incidental to such permitted uses.

The Act permits the use of campaign funds for mailings to constituents. Expenditures to seek office that are reasonably related to a political purpose, and expenditures associated with holding office that are reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose, are in compliance with the "public trust" provisions of Section 85202.3 (Section 85801.)

3 "All contributions ... shall be deemed to be held in trust for expenses associated with the election of the candidate to the specific office ..." (Section 85202(b).)

Depending on the circumstances, sending "information letters" to constituents in your supervisorial district would appear to constitute activity that was either related to seeking (reelection to) office or otherwise associated with holding office. While the Act prohibits the use of public funds to send many of these types of mailings (Section 89001; Regulation 18901 (copy enclosed)), it contemplates the use of campaign funds for such purposes.

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you requested. If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.
